read.subtitle: A cleaned reading of Game Theory #14
read.voice_policy: Direct argumentative prose derived from the lecture. Avoid repeated third-person framing. Do not impersonate first-person authorship except when quoting verified source material.
read.trace_policy: Paragraph-level transcript refs. Speculative claims stay marked as speculative.
read.dek: The lecture names the law of proximity: people and nations play many games at once, but the nearest game is the one that governs action. The Iran war is therefore not only Iran versus Israel or America. It is American factions, Israeli factions, and Iranian factions using the outside war to fight the civil war closest to them.
read.thesis: The law of proximity turns geopolitics inside out. Leader-killing matters because it removes the off-ramp and invites internal factions to weaponize foreign enemies. Tel Aviv's destruction can look like messianic evidence to Jerusalem; an Iran strike can become an American election tool; a global war can become a bailout fight between finance and AI. By the end, the same model reaches the soul: modernity overfed the animal soul, and economic rupture may force a return to spirituality, family, and meaning.

opening.heading: Core Reading
opening.text: Start with the strange reaction to destruction. Tel Aviv is being hit, Iran is attacking energy infrastructure, and the ordinary geopolitical question would be how the war ends. Jiang says that is the wrong first lens. Some Israelis are not simply afraid; they are excited because destruction looks like the weather of the Messiah. From there the lecture builds its machine: the nearest conflict is the one that rules. The world thinks it is watching an interstate war, but the decisive games are closer, inside parties, factions, religious cities, finance bubbles, and souls.

beats.heading: The Law Of Proximity
beats.summary: The nearest game dominates decision-making, so national behavior is often driven by internal factional games.

paragraphs.text: The law is simple: everyone plays many games at once, but the game closest to you shapes action most. You play a family game, a school game, a work game, a city game, a national game. The visible, immediate game wins attention. The same is true of nations. We think the game is between nations, but often the deciding game is within nations.
